title: OS Command Injection
principle: Don't build a shell command string from input. Call the program directly with its arguments passed as a list, and keep the shell out of it — no shell means no shell metacharacters to abuse. If a library already does the job in-process, prefer it over shelling out at all.

snippets.library: subprocess with a list argv and shell=False
snippets.note: Pass argv as a list; the default shell=False means host can't inject ; | && $(). Validate host against an allowlist/regex if it feeds a flag.

snippets.library: child_process.execFile / spawn with an args array
snippets.note: exec runs a shell and is the sink; execFile/spawn take (file, argsArray) and never invoke a shell. Avoid { shell: true }.
ProcessBuilder with separate arguments does not use a shell. Never build one string and never call exec via sh -c.
exec.Command(name, args...) does not invoke a shell, so host is a single argument. The bug is reintroducing a shell with sh -c and a concatenated string.

references.title: OWASP OS Command Injection Defense Cheat Sheet
